skill.name: xlsx-official
skill.description: Create, edit, analyze, and validate Excel workbooks with guidance for formulas, formatting, data analysis, and LibreOffice-based recalculation checks.
skill.summary: Build and verify Excel workbooks using pandas, openpyxl, formulas, formatting, and recalculation checks.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many high-risk-looking patterns, but most are false positives from Markdown examples, spreadsheet cell references, and license URLs. The confirmed concern is recalc.py, which writes a LibreOffice macro into the user profile and launches local LibreOffice-related commands; this is legitimate for workbook recalculation but needs a marketplace warning.

medium_findings.title: Persistent LibreOffice Macro Setup
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. recalc.py creates LibreOffice profile directories and writes Module1.xba under the user profile to install a recalculation macro. This matches the skill purpose, but persistent application-profile changes can affect future LibreOffice runs and should be disclosed. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The file write and hidden-profile path are explicit, and the macro contents are visible and limited to recalculation and save operations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file write and hidden-profile path are explicit, and the macro contents are visible and limited to recalculation and save operations.

medium_findings.title: Local External Command Execution
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE. recalc.py launches soffice and optionally gtimeout or timeout to recalculate workbooks. The subprocess calls do not use shell=True, and arguments are structured, but the skill still depends on executing local binaries against user-provided workbook paths. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The subprocess calls are clear and functional, but I did not find evidence of command injection because commands are passed as argument arrays.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are clear and functional, but I did not find evidence of command injection because commands are passed as argument arrays.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reference URLs Do Not Fetch Data
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The URLs in LICENSE.txt are legal references, and the URL in recalc.py is an XML namespace inside static macro content. No evidence found of network requests, downloads, or data exfiltration. Confidence: 0.97. Confidence reasoning: The inspected lines contain plain text URLs or XML metadata only, with no HTTP client usage in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected lines contain plain text URLs or XML metadata only, with no HTTP client usage in the skill files.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Examples Are Not Hidden Execution
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The SKILL.md findings are documentation examples using inline code and fenced Python, Bash, and JSON blocks. They instruct spreadsheet workflows but are not executable code inside the skill package. Confidence: 0.92. Confidence reasoning: The suspicious lines are inside Markdown instructions and examples, while executable subprocess behavior is confined to recalc.py.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious lines are inside Markdown instructions and examples, while executable subprocess behavior is confined to recalc.py.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Spreadsheet Terms Triggered Blocker Rules
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE. The C2 keyword is a spreadsheet cell reference, weak-crypto hits are ordinary words or documentation terms, and reconnaissance hits are code-style guidance. No evidence found of command-and-control, cryptography misuse, host profiling, or prompt injection. Confidence: 0.94. Confidence reasoning: The flagged text is semantically tied to formulas, spreadsheet examples, and style guidance rather than malware behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged text is semantically tied to formulas, spreadsheet examples, and style guidance rather than malware behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User Profile Macro File Write
dangerous_patterns.description: recalc.py writes a StarBasic macro file into the LibreOffice user profile. This is a legitimate recalculation mechanism, but it is a persistent filesystem change. Confidence: 0.86. Confidence reasoning: The path construction and write call are explicit in recalc.py.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path construction and write call are explicit in recalc.py.

dangerous_patterns.title: Subprocess-Based Office Recalculation
dangerous_patterns.description: recalc.py invokes LibreOffice through subprocess.run to recalculate formulas. It avoids shell=True, but still executes local programs and processes user-selected workbook files. Confidence: 0.78. Confidence reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct, while injection evidence is not present.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The subprocess calls are direct, while injection evidence is not present.

content.value_statement: Spreadsheet work often needs formulas, formatting, and checks across Excel files. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through workbook creation, editing, recalculation, and error review.
actual_capabilities: Guides creation of new .xlsx workbooks with openpyxl formulas and formatting.
Explains how to edit existing workbooks while preserving formulas and worksheet structure.
Uses pandas guidance for reading, analyzing, and exporting spreadsheet data.
Defines formula construction rules for dynamic, updateable Excel models.
Provides a recalc.py workflow to recalculate formulas and report Excel formula errors.
Includes financial model formatting conventions for inputs, formulas, links, and assumptions.
limitations: Formula recalculation requires LibreOffice and local command execution.
The helper script can create or update a LibreOffice macro in the user profile.
It does not replace manual review for complex financial models or regulated workbooks.
Large workbooks may be slow because the checker scans all cells.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Financial Model
use_cases.description: Create workbook tabs, assumptions, formulas, and formatting that follow common finance conventions.

use_cases.title: Clean and Export Spreadsheet Data
use_cases.description: Read Excel sheets with pandas, inspect columns, clean values, and export a usable workbook.

use_cases.title: Validate Workbook Formulas
use_cases.description: Run recalculation checks and locate formula errors before sharing an Excel file.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Excel workbook from this table. Add clear headers, basic formatting, and formulas for totals where needed.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Spreadsheet Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this workbook with pandas. Summarize the sheets, identify data quality issues, and create a cleaned Excel output.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Model
prompt_templates.prompt: Update this Excel model while preserving its existing styles. Add the new assumptions, formulas, and checks without hardcoded calculations.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Formula Workbook
prompt_templates.prompt: Recalculate this workbook, inspect formula errors, explain any failures, and revise the file until the workbook has no formula errors.

output_examples.input: Create a monthly sales workbook with revenue, cost, margin, and totals.
output_examples.output: A formatted Excel workbook with monthly columns, formula-based totals, margin percentages, and clearly marked input cells.

output_examples.input: Review an uploaded workbook for broken formulas.
output_examples.output: A concise validation report listing error types, affected sheets, example cell locations, and recommended fixes.

output_examples.input: Convert raw transaction data into a summary workbook.
output_examples.output: A cleaned workbook with normalized fields, summary tables, number formats, and notes about data quality issues.

best_practices: Use formulas for calculated values so the workbook updates when source data changes.
Run recalculation and formula-error checks before delivering files that contain formulas.
Keep assumptions in dedicated cells and document sources for hardcoded inputs.
anti_patterns: Do not hardcode calculated totals, growth rates, averages, or ratios into output cells.
Do not save a formula workbook after loading it with data-only values.
Do not ignore division-by-zero, missing references, or cross-sheet reference errors.

faq.question: What spreadsheet formats does this skill focus on?
faq.answer: It focuses on Excel workbook workflows for .xlsx files and related spreadsheet analysis tasks.

faq.question: Does it use pandas or openpyxl?
faq.answer: Yes. It recommends pandas for data analysis and openpyxl for formulas, formatting, and workbook structure.

faq.question: Why does it mention LibreOffice?
faq.answer: LibreOffice is used by recalc.py to recalculate formulas and update cached formula results in workbooks.

faq.question: Does the helper script change my system?
faq.answer: It can create a LibreOffice macro file in your user profile to support formula recalculation.

faq.question: Can it guarantee every financial model is correct?
faq.answer: No. It can guide construction and checks, but human review is still needed for business logic and assumptions.

faq.question: Is this suitable for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
